documents.name: rivets
documents.content:
# Rivets — Issue Tracking

These projects use **rivets** for issue tracking, not Jira/GitHub Issues/Linear.
Rivets is a Git-native CLI issue tracker that stores issues as JSONL in the repo
itself (`.rivets/issues.jsonl`). Issues branch, diff, and merge with the code.

When the user refers to "issues," "tickets," "tracking," "what's ready," or
"where are we" on a task, reach for `rivets` — do **not** assume an external
tracker.

## Detecting a rivets repo

A project uses rivets if it contains a `.rivets/` directory with `config.yaml`
and `issues.jsonl`. Check `rivets info` to confirm and see the issue prefix +
counts. The CLI operates on the `.rivets/` data of the current working
directory.

## Issue model

- **ID format:** `<prefix>-<hash>`, lowercase (e.g. `rivets-xov3`). The prefix
  comes from `.rivets/config.yaml` (`issue-prefix`). IDs are **not** sequential
  numbers like `RIVETS-1`.
- **Types:** `bug`, `feature`, `task`, `epic`, `chore` (default `task`).
- **Status:** `open`, `in_progress`, `blocked`, `closed`.
- **Priority:** integer `0`–`4` where **0=critical, 1=high, 2=medium, 3=low,
  4=backlog** (default `2`). Lower number = higher priority.
- **Dependency types:** `blocks` (hard blocker), `related` (soft link),
  `parent-child` (epic→task), `discovered-from` (found during work).

## Core commands

```bash
rivets info                      # Repo info: data path, prefix, issue counts
rivets stats                     # Project statistics

# Find work
rivets ready --limit 10          # Unblocked issues, ready to pick up
rivets blocked                   # Issues blocked by dependencies
rivets list                      # All non-closed issues (default sort: priority)
rivets list --status in_progress # Filter by status
rivets list --type feature -l <label>
rivets stale --days 30           # Issues untouched for N days

# Inspect
rivets show <id> [<id>...]       # Full detail: description, design, acceptance, deps

# Create
rivets create --title "..." --type feature -p 1 \
  -D "description" --design "..." --acceptance "..." \
  -l "label1,label2" --deps "blocks:rivets-abc"

# Update / lifecycle
rivets update <id>... --status in_progress
rivets update <id> -p 1 --title "..." --notes "..."
rivets close <id>... -r "Implemented in commit <hash>"
rivets reopen <id>... -r "reason"
rivets delete <id>               # Permanent
```

## Dependencies (mind the direction)

`rivets dep add <FROM> <TO>` means **FROM depends on TO** — i.e. TO blocks FROM.
So to say "rivets-aaa is blocked by rivets-bbb":

```bash
rivets dep add rivets-aaa rivets-bbb --type blocks   # bbb must finish before aaa
rivets dep remove <FROM> <TO>
rivets dep list <id>
rivets dep tree <id> [--depth N]   # depth 0 = unlimited
```

## Labels

Label comes first, then the issue id (or `--ids` for several):

```bash
rivets label add <label> <id>
rivets label add <label> --ids rivets-aaa rivets-bbb
rivets label remove <label> <id>
rivets label list <id>           # labels on one issue
rivets label list-all            # all labels in use
```

## Automation

Every command accepts `--json` for scripting:

```bash
rivets list --json | jq '.[] | select(.priority == 0)'
rivets ready --json
```

## Working a task (dogfooding workflow)

1. `rivets ready --limit 10` → pick an issue (or `rivets blocked` if nothing ready).
2. `rivets show <id>` → review description / design / acceptance criteria.
3. `rivets update <id> --status in_progress`, then branch `<type>/<desc>`
   (bug→`fix/`, feature→`feature/`, task/chore→`chore/`).
4. Implement + test.
5. `rivets close <id> -r "Implemented in commit <short-hash>"`.
6. `rivets ready` again to see what the close unblocked.

## Notes

- Run rivets commands from the repo whose issues you mean to read/modify; it
  resolves `.rivets/` relative to the working directory.
- The README's `rivets dep RIVETS-2 --blocks ...` and
  `rivets label add RIVETS-1 urgent` examples are outdated — use the syntax
  above (verified against `rivets <cmd> --help`).

documents.name: rust-rules
documents.content:
# Rust Enforcement Rules

Non-negotiable rules for any Rust work. These are the "what" — apply them on
every edit. For the "how" and worked examples, the `rust-best-practices` skill
has deep-dives.

## Error Handling
- No bare `.unwrap()`/`.expect()` in production code (tests are exempt). Use `?`, `match`, `unwrap_or`, or `ok_or()?`.
- No `let _ =` on a `Result` — log it (`if let Err(e) = ...`) or propagate it.
- No `.ok()?` / `filter_map(Result::ok)` / `let...else { continue }` that collapses distinct failure modes. Ask: does the caller need to know *which* failure this was?
- Log before returning `None` from a fallible function — silent `None` hides the cause.
- Return `Err` for invalid input, not an empty collection that looks like success.
- Match error *types/variants*, not `.to_string().contains(...)`. If the right variant doesn't exist, add it.
- Wrap internal errors to match the function's documented error contract — don't leak a helper's error variant.
- Distinguish "missing" (expected) from "corrupt" (bug) — don't collapse them.

## Trust Boundaries
- Treat deserialized data (JSON/YAML/TOML, manifests, frontmatter) as untrusted.
- Validate before *every* filesystem/network use, not at a single downstream checkpoint.
- Prefer parse-don't-validate: wrap invariants in a newtype with a fallible constructor over a free `validate_x(&T)` nothing calls.

## Paths
- Canonicalize paths at system boundaries; store/compare a consistent form.
- Document path contracts in types (absolute? relative to what?).
- `Path::is_absolute()` is platform-dependent — add explicit prefix checks when parsing input that may come from another platform.

## File I/O
- Atomic writes for important files (`NamedTempFile` → `persist`).
- Create parent directories before writing.

## Type Safety
- Constants or enums for fixed value sets; newtypes for distinct IDs.
- Validate immediately in setters/constructors; `debug_assert!` invariants in constructors.
- Match enum variants exhaustively for protocol/error/event enums — avoid `_ =>` catch-alls so new variants force a decision.

## Concurrency
- Handle mutex poisoning explicitly (recover via `into_inner()`), don't silently drop with `if let Ok(...)`.
- Prefer `try_borrow_mut()` over `borrow_mut()` for defensive `RefCell` access.

## Tests
- Every new public fn gets a happy-path test plus edge cases (empty/None/boundary).

## Before declaring work done
- `cargo fmt`
- `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` (clean)
- `cargo test`

documents.name: architecture-selection
documents.content:
# Architecture Selection

When the user's message starts with /architecture-selection, perform a systematic architecture analysis on the current feature's requirements.md and then proceed directly to design.

Strip the /architecture-selection prefix from the user's message. If a feature name is provided, use it; otherwise infer it from the most recently active spec in .kiro/specs/.

## Step 1: Determine Starting Point

Skip ALL entry point selection and workflow type questions. Do not ask the user whether this is a feature, bugfix, design-first, etc.

Strip the /architecture-selection prefix. Use the remainder as the feature description or feature name.

Check `.kiro/specs/` for an existing spec matching the feature name:

**Case A — No spec exists (new feature description provided):**
Invoke the feature-requirements-first-workflow subagent with preset "requirements" using the feature description as the prompt. Append: "SLASH_COMMAND_MODE: Do NOT use the userInput tool for any questions. Make all decisions autonomously." The subagent runs normally and shows its work in chat — it just won't pause to ask clarifying questions. Wait for requirements.md to be created, then proceed to Step 2.

**Case B — Spec exists with requirements.md (not yet designed):**
Proceed directly to Step 2 using requirements.md as input.

**Case C — Spec exists with both requirements.md and design.md:**
Proceed directly to Step 2. After writing architecture_selection.md, inform the user it has been written and suggest they update design.md to align with the selected architecture if needed. Do not re-invoke the design subagent.

**Case D — Spec exists with bugfix.md:**
Let the user know that architecture selection is designed for feature workflows and may not be meaningful for bugfixes. Ask if they'd like to proceed anyway or stop.

## Step 2: Architecture Analysis

Write your full analysis to `.kiro/specs/{feature_name}/architecture_analysis.md` as you work through each step below. This file is your scratchpad — it will be deleted after Step 3. Do not output analysis to the chat window.

### 2a. Variable Classification
Identify every logical variable implied by the acceptance criteria. Classify each as: Feature Flag, Configuration Parameter, Input, State, Event, Output, or Side Effect. Write as a table in architecture_analysis.md.

### 2b. Requirement–Variable Map
Build a matrix showing which variables each REQ reads (R) or writes (W). Identify clusters of variables that are frequently co-accessed — these are datatype candidates. Variables appearing across many requirements are potential coupling points. Write to architecture_analysis.md.

### 2c. Invariant Extraction
Extract invariants from the acceptance criteria (properties that must hold across all transitions). Classify each by which variables it constrains. Write to architecture_analysis.md.

### 2d. Generate 2–3 Candidate Architectures — BEFORE evaluating any of them

**CRITICAL: Fully describe ALL candidates first. Do not score or compare until all candidates are written.**

Produce genuinely different candidates (e.g. entity-oriented, use-case-oriented, layer-oriented, event-driven). Let the variable clusters guide decomposition — do not anchor to familiar patterns from the workspace. **At least one candidate must include an explicit persistence/storage boundary as a separate component.**

For each candidate, write to architecture_analysis.md:
- Component inventory (name, owned state, responsibility)
- Information flow matrix (use → for calls, ← for callbacks, ↔ for bidirectional)
- Requirement allocation matrix
- Invariant allocation matrix
- **Steelman**: describe this candidate as its strongest advocate would — what does it do exceptionally well?

If all candidates look structurally similar, you have not explored the design space enough. Generate a genuinely different one.

### 2e. Score All Candidates

Only after all candidates are fully described, compute these metrics for each:
- Cross-cutting requirements %: REQ allocated to >1 component
- Cross-cutting invariants %: invariants requiring multi-component coordination
- Information flow density: edges / (N×(N-1))
- God object score: max % of state owned by one component
- Synchronous cycles: cycles in the directed flow graph (zero is ideal)
- Max fan-in / fan-out
- Evolvability cost: avg components whose interfaces change when one REQ is added

**Red flags:** sync cycles, any component owning >50% of state, REQs allocated to 3+ components, invariants requiring global coordination.

### 2f. Select Best Candidate
Compare all candidates side by side on the metrics. Default priority: minimize cross-cutting concerns and flow density. If the user stated preferences, weight accordingly.

Selection format: "Candidate X is recommended because [metric-tied reasons]. The trade-off is [what is sacrificed]. If [alternative priority] matters more, Candidate Y would be preferable because [reasons]."

After writing architecture_selection.md in Step 3, delete architecture_analysis.md.

## Step 3: Write architecture_selection.md

Write `.kiro/specs/{feature_name}/architecture_selection.md` with this structure:

```
# Architecture Selection: {feature_name}

## Recommended Architecture: {Candidate Name}

### Rationale
[2–3 sentences tied to metrics. Acknowledge the main trade-off.]

### Components
| Component | Owned State | Responsibility |
|-----------|-------------|----------------|
| ...       | ...         | ...            |

### Information Flow
| From \ To | Comp A | Comp B | Comp C |
|------------|--------|--------|--------|
| ...        |        |        |        |

### Requirement Allocation
| Requirement | Component(s) |
|-------------|--------------|
| REQ-i.j     | ...          |

### Key Design-Induced Invariants
[Invariants that arise from this architecture's partitioning decisions, not from requirements directly]

### Alternatives Considered
| Candidate | Strength | Weakness | Why Not Selected |
|-----------|----------|----------|-----------------|
| ...       | ...      | ...      | ...             |

### Metrics Summary
| Metric | Selected | Alt A | Alt B |
|--------|----------|-------|-------|
| Cross-cutting reqs % | | | |
| Cross-cutting invariants % | | | |
| Flow density | | | |
| God object score | | | |
| Sync cycles | | | |
| Max fan-in | | | |
| Max fan-out | | | |
| Evolvability cost | | | |
```

## Step 4: Proceed to Design

Immediately invoke the feature-requirements-first-workflow subagent with preset "design".

Append to the subagent prompt:
"ARCHITECTURE_CONTEXT: architecture_selection.md has been written. You MUST read .kiro/specs/{feature_name}/architecture_selection.md before writing the Architecture section of design.md. Use the recommended component breakdown and information flow as the basis for the Architecture section."

## After Design Is Created

Post a brief, readable summary in chat using this format:

---
**Architecture selected:** {Candidate Name}

**Components:**
- {ComponentA} — one-line responsibility
- {ComponentB} — one-line responsibility
- {ComponentC} — one-line responsibility

**Why this architecture:** [1–2 sentences on the key metric wins]
**Trade-off:** [1 sentence on what was sacrificed]

Full details in .kiro/specs/{feature_name}/architecture_selection.md
---

Then ask if the user is happy or wants to iterate.

## Iteration

If the user wants to change the architecture, re-run Steps 2–4 with their feedback. Do NOT stop between steps.

Example: "/architecture-selection my-feature"
→ read requirements.md
→ run analysis in-context
→ write architecture_selection.md
→ invoke feature-requirements-first-workflow preset "design" with ARCHITECTURE_CONTEXT appended
→ present summary

# Quick Spec

When the user's message starts with /quick-spec, skip ALL entry point selection steps and immediately generate all 3 spec docs automatically.

Strip the /quick-spec prefix from the user's message and use the remainder as the feature description.

## Workflow

Treat the request as a new feature using the requirements-first workflow:
1. Invoke feature-requirements-first-workflow subagent with preset "requirements"
2. Immediately invoke with preset "design" — do NOT wait for user input
3. Immediately invoke with preset "tasks" — do NOT wait for user input

Append this instruction to every subagent prompt:
"SLASH_COMMAND_MODE: Do NOT use the userInput tool for any questions. Make all decisions autonomously using sensible defaults: use Structured Pseudocode if no language is detected."

## After All 3 Docs Are Created

Present a brief summary and ask if the user is happy or wants to iterate.

## Iteration

When the user wants to change something (e.g. "make it simple"), update ALL 3 documents by invoking each phase sequentially with the user's feedback as the prompt (with SLASH_COMMAND_MODE appended). Do NOT stop between phases.

Example: "/quick-spec create a calculator app"
→ invoke feature-requirements-first-workflow preset "requirements" with prompt "create a calculator app. SLASH_COMMAND_MODE: ..."
→ immediately invoke preset "design"
→ immediately invoke preset "tasks"
→ present summary, ask if happy or want changes

## Workflow

1. Invoke bugfix-workflow subagent with preset "requirements"
2. Wait for requirements to complete, then invoke with preset "design"
3. After design completes, invoke with preset "tasks"

Append this instruction to every subagent prompt:
"SLASH_COMMAND_MODE: Do NOT use the userInput tool for spec-type or workflow-type selection. The spec type is Bug Fix and the workflow is requirements-first. You MAY still ask clarifying questions about the bug itself."

## After All 3 Docs Are Created

Present a brief summary and ask if the user is happy or wants to iterate.

## Iteration

When the user wants to change something, update ALL 3 documents by invoking each phase sequentially with the user's feedback as the prompt (with SLASH_COMMAND_MODE appended). Do NOT stop between phases.

Example: "/bug-fix login page crashes on empty email"
→ invoke bugfix-workflow preset "requirements" with prompt "login page crashes on empty email. SLASH_COMMAND_MODE: ..."
→ invoke preset "design"
→ invoke preset "tasks"
→ present summary, ask if happy or want changes

items.name: rust-best-practices
items.description:
**ALWAYS USE** when working with Rust code. This skill contains 45 rules that prevent common bugs.

Trigger on ANY of these:
- Editing .rs files or Cargo.toml
- Writing new Rust functions, structs, enums, or impl blocks
- Reviewing Rust code before committing
- Debugging Rust errors (borrow checker, Option/Result, lifetimes)
- Working with: unwrap, expect, ?, Option, Result, Path, PathBuf
- File I/O operations in Rust (read, write, atomic writes)
- CLI development with clap
- Error handling patterns
- Functions returning Option or Result that might fail silently
- Path operations (canonicalize, file_name, parent, extension)
- Deserializing JSON/YAML/TOML from external sources

Key rules to apply:
- Rule 35: Log before returning None (prevent silent failures)
- Rule 36: Return Err for invalid input, not empty collections
- Rule 37: Canonicalize paths at system boundaries
- Rule 39: Every public method needs tests
- Rule 40: Validate untrusted data at point of use
- Rule 41: Audit ergonomic error suppressors (.ok(), filter_map(Result::ok))
- Rule 42: Match error types, not error strings

**Before committing Rust code**, review the Pre-Commit Checklist in this skill.

items.name: rust-best-practices
items.description:
**ALWAYS USE** when working with Rust code. This skill contains 45 rules that prevent common bugs.

Trigger on ANY of these:
- Editing .rs files or Cargo.toml
- Writing new Rust functions, structs, enums, or impl blocks
- Reviewing Rust code before committing
- Debugging Rust errors (borrow checker, Option/Result, lifetimes)
- Working with: unwrap, expect, ?, Option, Result, Path, PathBuf
- File I/O operations in Rust (read, write, atomic writes)
- CLI development with clap
- Error handling patterns
- Functions returning Option or Result that might fail silently
- Path operations (canonicalize, file_name, parent, extension)
- Deserializing JSON/YAML/TOML from external sources

Key rules to apply:
- Rule 35: Log before returning None (prevent silent failures)
- Rule 36: Return Err for invalid input, not empty collections
- Rule 37: Canonicalize paths at system boundaries
- Rule 39: Every public method needs tests
- Rule 40: Validate untrusted data at point of use
- Rule 41: Audit ergonomic error suppressors (.ok(), filter_map(Result::ok))
- Rule 42: Match error types, not error strings

**Before committing Rust code**, review the Pre-Commit Checklist in this skill.

availableCommands.name: context-gatherer
availableCommands.description:
Analyzes repository structure to identify relevant files and content sections needed to address a user issue. Uses a specialized subagent to explore the codebase and provide focused context.

  When to use:
  - Whenever you need to understand, explore, or locate relevant content in the repository

  When NOT to use:
  - When you already know which specific files need to be modified
  - For simple single-file changes where the file path is already known

  Rules:
  1. Provide a clear, specific prompt for what context you need
  2. Include your intent in the prompt — what you're trying to accomplish, not just what you're looking for
  3. The subagent will analyze file structure and its content, and return relevant context automatically

tools.name: jira_get_user_profile
tools.description:
Retrieve profile information for a specific Jira user.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    user_identifier: User identifier (email, username, key, or account ID).

Returns:
    JSON string representing the Jira user profile object, or an error object if not found.

Raises:
    ValueError: If the Jira client is not configured or available.

user_identifier.description: Identifier for the user (e.g., email address 'user@example.com', username 'johndoe', account ID 'accountid:...', or key for Server/DC).

tools.name: jira_get_issue_watchers
tools.description:
Get the list of watchers for a Jira issue.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    issue_key: Jira issue key.

Returns:
    JSON string with watcher count and list of watchers.

Raises:
    ValueError: If the Jira client is not configured or available.

tools.name: jira_add_watcher
tools.description:
Add a user as a watcher to a Jira issue.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    issue_key: Jira issue key.
    user_identifier: Account ID (Cloud) or username (Server/DC).

Returns:
    JSON string with success confirmation.

Raises:
    ValueError: If the Jira client is not configured or available.

user_identifier.description: User to add as watcher. For Jira Cloud, use the account ID. For Jira Server/DC, use the username.

tools.name: jira_remove_watcher
tools.description:
Remove a user from watching a Jira issue.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    issue_key: Jira issue key.
    username: Username to remove (Server/DC).
    account_id: Account ID to remove (Cloud).

Returns:
    JSON string with success confirmation.

Raises:
    ValueError: If the Jira client is not configured or available.

account_id.description: Account ID to remove (for Jira Cloud).

tools.name: jira_get_issue
tools.description:
Get details of a specific Jira issue including its Epic links and relationship information.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    issue_key: Jira issue key.
    fields: Comma-separated list of fields to return (e.g., 'summary,status,customfield_10010'), a single field as a string (e.g., 'duedate'), '*all' for all fields, or omitted for essentials.
    expand: Optional fields to expand.
    comment_limit: Maximum number of comments.
    properties: Issue properties to return.
    update_history: Whether to update issue view history.

Returns:
    JSON string representing the Jira issue object.

Raises:
    ValueError: If the Jira client is not configured or available.

fields.description: (Optional) Comma-separated list of fields to return (e.g., 'summary,status,customfield_10010'). You may also provide a single field as a string (e.g., 'duedate'). Use '*all' for all fields (including custom fields), or omit for essential fields only.

expand.description: (Optional) Fields to expand. Examples: 'renderedFields' (for rendered content), 'transitions' (for available status transitions), 'changelog' (for history)

comment_limit.description: Maximum number of comments to include (0 or null for no comments)

properties.description: (Optional) A comma-separated list of issue properties to return

update_history.description: Whether to update the issue view history for the requesting user

tools.name: jira_search
tools.description:
Search Jira issues using JQL (Jira Query Language).

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    jql: JQL query string.
    fields: Comma-separated fields to return.
    limit: Maximum number of results.
    start_at: Starting index for pagination.
    projects_filter: Comma-separated list of project keys to filter by.
    expand: Optional fields to expand.
    page_token: Pagination token from a previous search result (Cloud only).

Returns:
    JSON string representing the search results including pagination info.

jql.description:
JQL query string (Jira Query Language). Examples:
- Find Epics: "issuetype = Epic AND project = PROJ"
- Find issues in Epic: "parent = PROJ-123"
- Find by status: "status = 'In Progress' AND project = PROJ"
- Find by assignee: "assignee = currentUser()"
- Find recently updated: "updated >= -7d AND project = PROJ"
- Find by label: "labels = frontend AND project = PROJ"
- Find by priority: "priority = High AND project = PROJ"

    return_limit: Cap on number of results after filtering.
    values_only: Return compact format with only value strings.

Returns:
    JSON string with the list of available options.

context_id.description: Field context ID (Cloud only). If omitted, auto-resolves to the global context.

tools.name: jira_update_proforma_form_answers
tools.description:
Update form field answers using the Jira Forms REST API.

This is the primary method for updating form data. Each answer object
must specify the question ID, answer type, and value.

**⚠️ KNOWN LIMITATION - DATETIME fields:**
The Jira Forms API does NOT properly preserve time components in DATETIME fields.
Only the date portion is stored; times are reset to midnight (00:00:00).

**Workaround for DATETIME fields:**
Use jira_update_issue to directly update the underlying custom fields instead:
1. Get the custom field ID from the form details (question's "jiraField" property)
2. Use jira_update_issue with fields like: {"customfield_XXXXX": "2026-01-09T11:50:00-08:00"}

Example:
```python
# Instead of updating via form (loses time):
# jira_update_proforma_form_answers(issue_key, form_id, [{"questionId": "91", "type": "DATETIME", "value": "..."}])

# Use direct field update (preserves time):
jira_update_issue(issue_key, {"customfield_10542": "2026-01-09T11:50:00-08:00"})
```

**Automatic DateTime Conversion:**
For DATE and DATETIME fields, you can provide values as:
- ISO 8601 strings (e.g., "2024-12-17T19:00:00Z", "2024-12-17")
- Unix timestamps in milliseconds (e.g., 1734465600000)

The tool automatically converts ISO 8601 strings to Unix timestamps.

Example answers:
[
    {"questionId": "q1", "type": "TEXT", "value": "Updated description"},
    {"questionId": "q2", "type": "SELECT", "value": "Product A"},
    {"questionId": "q3", "type": "NUMBER", "value": 42},
    {"questionId": "q4", "type": "DATE", "value": "2024-12-17"}
]

Common answer types:
- TEXT: String values
- NUMBER: Numeric values
- DATE: Date values (ISO 8601 string or Unix timestamp in ms)
- DATETIME: DateTime values - ⚠️ USE WORKAROUND ABOVE
- SELECT: Single selection from options
- MULTI_SELECT: Multiple selections (value as list)
- CHECKBOX: Boolean values

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    issue_key: The issue key containing the form.
    form_id: The form UUID (get from get_issue_proforma_forms).
    answers: List of answer objects with questionId, type, and value.

Returns:
    JSON string with operation result.

answers.description: List of answer objects. Each answer must have: questionId (string), type (TEXT/NUMBER/SELECT/etc), value (any)

    issue_key: The Jira issue key.
    include_status_changes: Whether to include status change history.
    include_status_summary: Whether to include aggregated time per status.

Returns:
    JSON string with issue dates and optional status tracking data.

include_status_changes.description: Include status change history with timestamps and durations

include_status_summary.description: Include aggregated time spent in each status

tools.name: jira_get_issue_sla
tools.description:
Calculate SLA metrics for a Jira issue.

Computes various time-based metrics including cycle time, lead time,
time spent in each status, due date compliance, and more.

Working hours can be configured via environment variables:
- JIRA_SLA_WORKING_HOURS_ONLY: Enable working hours filtering (true/false)
- JIRA_SLA_WORKING_HOURS_START: Start time (e.g., "09:00")
- JIRA_SLA_WORKING_HOURS_END: End time (e.g., "17:00")
- JIRA_SLA_WORKING_DAYS: Working days (e.g., "1,2,3,4,5" for Mon-Fri)
- JIRA_SLA_TIMEZONE: Timezone for calculations (e.g., "America/New_York")

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    issue_key: The Jira issue key.
    metrics: Comma-separated list of metrics to calculate.
    working_hours_only: Use working hours only for calculations.
    include_raw_dates: Include raw date values in response.

Returns:
    JSON string with calculated SLA metrics.

metrics.description: Comma-separated list of SLA metrics to calculate. Available: cycle_time, lead_time, time_in_status, due_date_compliance, resolution_time, first_response_time. Defaults to configured metrics or 'cycle_time,time_in_status'.

working_hours_only.description: Calculate using working hours only (excludes weekends/non-business hours). Defaults to value from JIRA_SLA_WORKING_HOURS_ONLY environment variable.

include_raw_dates.description: Include raw date values in the response

tools.name: jira_get_issue_development_info
tools.description:
Get development information (PRs, commits, branches) linked to a Jira issue.

This retrieves the development panel information that shows linked
pull requests, branches, and commits from connected source control systems
like Bitbucket, GitHub, or GitLab.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    issue_key: The Jira issue key.
    application_type: Optional filter by source control type.
    data_type: Optional filter by data type (pullrequest, branch, etc.).

Returns:
    JSON string with development information including:
    - pullRequests: List of linked pull requests with status, author, reviewers
    - branches: List of linked branches
    - commits: List of linked commits
    - repositories: List of repositories involved

application_type.description: (Optional) Filter by application type. Examples: 'stash' (Bitbucket Server), 'bitbucket', 'github', 'gitlab'

tools.name: jira_get_issues_development_info
tools.description:
Get development information for multiple Jira issues.

Batch retrieves development panel information (PRs, commits, branches)
for multiple issues at once.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    issue_keys: List of Jira issue keys.
    application_type: Optional filter by source control type.
    data_type: Optional filter by data type.

Returns:
    JSON string with list of development information for each issue.

issue_keys.description: Comma-separated list of Jira issue keys (e.g., 'PROJ-123,PROJ-456')

application_type.description: (Optional) Filter by application type. Examples: 'stash' (Bitbucket Server), 'bitbucket', 'github', 'gitlab'

tools.name: confluence_search
tools.description:
Search Confluence content using simple terms or CQL.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    query: Search query - can be simple text or a CQL query string.
    limit: Maximum number of results (1-50).
    spaces_filter: Comma-separated list of space keys to filter by.

Returns:
    JSON string representing a list of simplified Confluence page objects.

query.description:
Search query - can be either a simple text (e.g. 'project documentation') or a CQL query string. Simple queries use 'siteSearch' by default, to mimic the WebUI search, with an automatic fallback to 'text' search if not supported. Examples of CQL:
- Basic search: 'type=page AND space=DEV'
- Personal space search: 'space="~username"' (note: personal space keys starting with ~ must be quoted)
- Search by title: 'title~"Meeting Notes"'
- Use siteSearch: 'siteSearch ~ "important concept"'
- Use text search: 'text ~ "important concept"'
- Recent content: 'created >= "2023-01-01"'
- Content with specific label: 'label=documentation'
- Recently modified content: 'lastModified > startOfMonth("-1M")'
- Content modified this year: 'creator = currentUser() AND lastModified > startOfYear()'
- Content you contributed to recently: 'contributor = currentUser() AND lastModified > startOfWeek()'
- Content watched by user: 'watcher = "user@domain.com" AND type = page'
- Exact phrase in content: 'text ~ "\"Urgent Review Required\"" AND label = "pending-approval"'
- Title wildcards: 'title ~ "Minutes*" AND (space = "HR" OR space = "Marketing")'
Note: Special identifiers need proper quoting in CQL: personal space keys (e.g., "~username"), reserved words, numeric IDs, and identifiers with special characters.

spaces_filter.description: (Optional) Comma-separated list of space keys to filter results by. Overrides the environment variable CONFLUENCE_SPACES_FILTER if provided. Use empty string to disable filtering.

tools.name: confluence_get_page
tools.description:
Get content of a specific Confluence page by its ID, or by its title and space key.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    page_id: Confluence page ID. If provided, 'title' and 'space_key' are ignored.
    title: The exact title of the page. Must be used with 'space_key'.
    space_key: The key of the space. Must be used with 'title'.
    include_metadata: Whether to include page metadata.
    convert_to_markdown: Convert content to markdown (true) or keep raw HTML (false).

Returns:
    JSON string representing the page content and/or metadata, or an error if not found or parameters are invalid.

page_id.description: Confluence page ID (numeric ID, can be found in the page URL). For example, in the URL 'https://example.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/TEAM/pages/123456789/Page+Title', the page ID is '123456789'. Provide this OR both 'title' and 'space_key'. If page_id is provided, title and space_key will be ignored.

title.description: The exact title of the Confluence page. Use this with 'space_key' if 'page_id' is not known.

space_key.description: The key of the Confluence space where the page resides (e.g., 'DEV', 'TEAM'). Required if using 'title'.

include_metadata.description: Whether to include page metadata such as creation date, last update, version, and labels.

convert_to_markdown.description: Whether to convert page to markdown (true) or keep it in raw HTML format (false). Raw HTML can reveal macros (like dates) not visible in markdown, but CAUTION: using HTML significantly increases token usage in AI responses.

tools.name: confluence_get_page_children
tools.description:
Get child pages and folders of a specific Confluence page.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    parent_id: The ID of the parent page.
    expand: Fields to expand.
    limit: Maximum number of child items.
    include_content: Whether to include page content.
    convert_to_markdown: Convert content to markdown if include_content is true.
    start: Starting index for pagination.
    include_folders: Whether to include child folders (default: True).

Returns:
    JSON string representing a list of child page and folder objects.

parent_id.description: The ID of the parent page whose children you want to retrieve

expand.description: Fields to expand in the response (e.g., 'version', 'body.storage')

limit.description: Maximum number of child items to return (1-50)

include_content.description: Whether to include the page content in the response

convert_to_markdown.description: Whether to convert page content to markdown (true) or keep it in raw HTML format (false). Only relevant if include_content is true.

include_folders.description: Whether to include child folders in addition to child pages

tools.name: confluence_get_space_page_tree
tools.description:
Get page hierarchy for a Confluence space as a flat list.

Returns pages with parent_id and depth attributes for token-efficient
processing. Filter by depth to focus on relevant sections, or find
pages by title. Much more efficient than rendering full ASCII trees.

Use this to understand space organization before creating/moving pages.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    space_key: Space key identifier.
    limit: Maximum pages to fetch (start with 100 for faster results).

Returns:
    JSON with space_key, total_pages, and pages array containing
    {id, title, parent_id, position, depth} for each page.
    Root pages have parent_id: null and depth: 0.

tools.name: confluence_get_comments
tools.description:
Get comments for a specific Confluence page.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    page_id: Confluence page ID.

Returns:
    JSON string representing a list of comment objects.

page_id.description: Confluence page ID (numeric ID, can be parsed from URL, e.g. from 'https://example.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/TEAM/pages/123456789/Page+Title' -> '123456789')

tools.name: confluence_get_labels
tools.description:
Get labels for Confluence content (pages, blog posts, or attachments).

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    page_id: Confluence content ID (page or attachment).

Returns:
    JSON string representing a list of label objects.

page_id.description: Confluence content ID (page, blog post, or attachment). For pages: numeric ID from URL (e.g., '123456789'). For attachments: ID with 'att' prefix (e.g., 'att123456789'). Works with any Confluence content type that supports labels.

tools.name: confluence_add_label
tools.description:
Add label to Confluence content (pages, blog posts, or attachments).

Useful for:
- Categorizing attachments (e.g., 'screenshot', 'diagram', 'legal-doc')
- Tracking status (e.g., 'approved', 'needs-review', 'archived')
- Filtering content by topic or version

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    page_id: Content ID (page or attachment).
    name: Label name to add.

Returns:
    JSON string representing the updated list of label objects.

Raises:
    ValueError: If in read-only mode or Confluence client is unavailable.

page_id.description: Confluence content ID to label. For pages/blogs: numeric ID (e.g., '123456789'). For attachments: ID with 'att' prefix (e.g., 'att123456789'). Use get_attachments to find attachment IDs.

name.description: Label name to add (lowercase, no spaces). Examples: 'draft', 'reviewed', 'confidential', 'v1.0'. Labels help organize and categorize content.

tools.name: confluence_create_page
tools.description:
Create a new Confluence page.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    space_key: The key of the space.
    title: The title of the page.
    content: The content of the page (format depends on content_format).
    parent_id: Optional parent page ID.
    content_format: The format of the content ('markdown', 'wiki', or 'storage').
    enable_heading_anchors: Whether to enable heading anchors (markdown only).
    include_content: Whether to include page content in the response.
    emoji: Optional page title emoji (icon shown in navigation).

Returns:
    JSON string representing the created page object.

Raises:
    ValueError: If in read-only mode, Confluence client is unavailable, or invalid content_format.

space_key.description: The key of the space to create the page in (usually a short uppercase code like 'DEV', 'TEAM', or 'DOC')

content.description: The content of the page. Format depends on content_format parameter. Can be Markdown (default), wiki markup, or storage format

parent_id.description: (Optional) parent page ID. If provided, this page will be created as a child of the specified page

content_format.description: (Optional) The format of the content parameter. Options: 'markdown' (default), 'wiki', or 'storage'. Wiki format uses Confluence wiki markup syntax

enable_heading_anchors.description: (Optional) Whether to enable automatic heading anchor generation. Only applies when content_format is 'markdown'

include_content.description: (Optional) Whether to include page content in the response. Defaults to false since callers already have the content at create time

emoji.description: (Optional) Page title emoji (icon shown in navigation). Can be any emoji character like '📝', '🚀', '📚'. Set to null/None to remove.

tools.name: confluence_update_page
tools.description:
Update an existing Confluence page.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    page_id: The ID of the page to update.
    title: The new title of the page.
    content: The new content of the page (format depends on content_format).
    is_minor_edit: Whether this is a minor edit.
    version_comment: Optional comment for this version.
    parent_id: Optional new parent page ID.
    content_format: The format of the content ('markdown', 'wiki', or 'storage').
    enable_heading_anchors: Whether to enable heading anchors (markdown only).
    include_content: Whether to include page content in the response.
    emoji: Optional page title emoji (icon shown in navigation).

Returns:
    JSON string representing the updated page object.

Raises:
    ValueError: If Confluence client is not configured, available, or invalid content_format.

page_id.description: The ID of the page to update

title.description: The new title of the page

content.description: The new content of the page. Format depends on content_format parameter

is_minor_edit.description: Whether this is a minor edit

parent_id.description: Optional the new parent page ID

content_format.description: (Optional) The format of the content parameter. Options: 'markdown' (default), 'wiki', or 'storage'. Wiki format uses Confluence wiki markup syntax

enable_heading_anchors.description: (Optional) Whether to enable automatic heading anchor generation. Only applies when content_format is 'markdown'

include_content.description: (Optional) Whether to include page content in the response. Defaults to false since callers already have the content at update time

emoji.description: (Optional) Page title emoji (icon shown in navigation). Can be any emoji character like '📝', '🚀', '📚'. Set to null/None to remove.

tools.name: confluence_delete_page
tools.description:
Delete an existing Confluence page.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    page_id: The ID of the page to delete.

Returns:
    JSON string indicating success or failure.

Raises:
    ValueError: If Confluence client is not configured or available.

page_id.description: The ID of the page to delete

tools.name: confluence_move_page
tools.description:
Move a Confluence page to a new parent or space.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    page_id: The ID of the page to move.
    target_parent_id: Target parent page ID.
    target_space_key: Target space key for cross-space moves.
    position: Position relative to target ('append', 'above', or 'below').

Returns:
    JSON string representing the moved page object.

Raises:
    ValueError: If neither target_parent_id nor target_space_key
        is provided, or if Confluence client is not configured.

page_id.description: ID of the page to move

target_parent_id.description: Target parent page ID. If omitted with target_space_key, moves to space root.

target_space_key.description: Target space key for cross-space moves

position.description: Position: 'append' (default, move as child of target), 'above' (move before target as sibling), or 'below' (move after target as sibling)

tools.name: confluence_add_comment
tools.description:
Add a comment to a Confluence page.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    page_id: The ID of the page to add a comment to.
    body: The comment content in Markdown format.

Returns:
    JSON string representing the created comment.

Raises:
    ValueError: If in read-only mode or Confluence client is unavailable.

page_id.description: The ID of the page to add a comment to

body.description: The comment content in Markdown format

tools.name: confluence_reply_to_comment
tools.description:
Reply to an existing comment thread on a Confluence page.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    comment_id: The ID of the parent comment to reply to.
    body: The reply content in Markdown format.

Returns:
    JSON string representing the created reply comment.

Raises:
    ValueError: If in read-only mode or Confluence client is unavailable.

comment_id.description: The ID of the parent comment to reply to

body.description: The reply content in Markdown format

tools.name: confluence_search_user
tools.description:
Search Confluence users using CQL (Cloud) or group member API (Server/DC).

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    query: Search query - a CQL query string for user search.
    limit: Maximum number of results (1-50).
    group_name: Group to search within on Server/DC.

Returns:
    JSON string representing a list of simplified Confluence user search result objects.

query.description:
Search query - a CQL query string for user search. Examples of CQL:
- Basic user lookup by full name: 'user.fullname ~ "First Last"'
Note: Special identifiers need proper quoting in CQL: personal space keys (e.g., "~username"), reserved words, numeric IDs, and identifiers with special characters.

group_name.description: Group to search within on Server/DC instances (default: 'confluence-users'). Ignored on Cloud.

tools.name: confluence_get_page_history
tools.description:
Get a historical version of a specific Confluence page.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    page_id: Confluence page ID.
    version: The version number to retrieve.
    convert_to_markdown: Convert content to markdown (true) or keep raw HTML (false).

Returns:
    JSON string representing the page content at the specified version.

page_id.description: Confluence page ID (numeric ID, can be found in the page URL). For example, in 'https://example.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/TEAM/pages/123456789/Page+Title', the page ID is '123456789'.

version.description: The version number of the page to retrieve

convert_to_markdown.description: Whether to convert page to markdown (true) or keep it in raw HTML format (false). Raw HTML can reveal macros (like dates) not visible in markdown, but CAUTION: using HTML significantly increases token usage in AI responses.

tools.name: confluence_get_page_diff
tools.description:
Get a unified diff between two versions of a Confluence page.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    page_id: Confluence page ID.
    from_version: Source version number.
    to_version: Target version number.

Returns:
    JSON string with page info and unified diff.

page_id.description: Confluence page ID (numeric ID, can be found in the page URL). For example, in 'https://example.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/TEAM/pages/123456789/Page+Title', the page ID is '123456789'.

tools.name: confluence_get_page_views
tools.description:
Get view statistics for a Confluence page.

Note: This tool is only available for Confluence Cloud. Server/Data Center
instances do not support the Analytics API.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    page_id: The Confluence page ID.
    include_title: Whether to include the page title in the response.

Returns:
    JSON string with page view statistics including total views and last viewed date.

page_id.description: Confluence page ID (numeric ID, can be found in the page URL). For example, in 'https://example.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/TEAM/pages/123456789/Page+Title', the page ID is '123456789'.

include_title.description: Whether to fetch and include the page title

tools.name: confluence_upload_attachment
tools.description:
Upload an attachment to Confluence content (page or blog post).

If the attachment already exists (same filename), a new version is created.
This is useful for:
- Attaching documents, images, or files to a page
- Updating existing attachments with new versions
- Adding supporting materials to documentation

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    content_id: The ID of the content to attach to.
    file_path: Path to the file to upload.
    comment: Optional comment for the attachment.
    minor_edit: Whether this is a minor edit (no notifications).

Returns:
    JSON string with upload confirmation and attachment metadata.

content_id.description: The ID of the Confluence content (page or blog post) to attach the file to. Page IDs can be found in the page URL or by using the search/get_page tools. Example: '123456789'

file_path.description: Full path to the file to upload. Can be absolute (e.g., '/home/user/document.pdf' or 'C:\Users\name\file.docx') or relative to the current working directory (e.g., './uploads/document.pdf'). If a file with the same name already exists, a new version will be created.

comment.description: (Optional) A comment describing this attachment or version. Visible in the attachment history. Example: 'Updated Q4 2024 figures'

minor_edit.description: (Optional) Whether this is a minor edit. If true, watchers are not notified. Default is false.

tools.name: confluence_upload_attachments
tools.description:
Upload multiple attachments to Confluence content in a single operation.

More efficient than calling upload_attachment multiple times. If files with the
same names exist, new versions are created automatically.

Useful for:
- Bulk uploading documentation assets (diagrams, screenshots, etc.)
- Adding multiple related files to a page at once
- Batch updating existing attachments with new versions

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    content_id: The ID of the content to attach to.
    file_paths: List of file paths to upload.
    comment: Optional comment for the attachments.
    minor_edit: Whether this is a minor edit.

Returns:
    JSON string with upload results for each file.

content_id.description: The ID of the Confluence content (page or blog post) to attach files to. Example: '123456789'. If uploading multiple files with the same names, new versions will be created automatically.

file_paths.description: Comma-separated list of file paths to upload. Can be absolute or relative paths. Examples: './file1.pdf,./file2.png' or 'C:\docs\report.docx,D:\image.jpg'. All files uploaded with same comment/minor_edit settings.

comment.description: (Optional) Comment for all uploaded attachments. Visible in version history. Example: 'Q4 2024 batch upload'

minor_edit.description: (Optional) Whether this is a minor edit. If true, watchers are not notified. Default is false.

tools.name: confluence_get_attachments
tools.description:
List all attachments for a Confluence content item (page or blog post).

Returns metadata about attachments including:
- Attachment ID, title, and file type
- File size and download URL
- Creation/modification dates
- Version information

**Important**: Confluence API returns 'application/octet-stream' as the media type
for most binary files (PNG, JPG, PDF) instead of specific types like 'image/png'.
For filtering by file type, using the 'filename' parameter is more reliable
(e.g., filename='*.png' pattern matching if supported, or exact filename).

Useful for:
- Discovering what files are attached to a page
- Getting attachment IDs for download operations
- Checking if a specific file exists
- Listing images/documents for processing

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    content_id: The ID of the content.
    start: Starting index for pagination.
    limit: Maximum number of results (1-100).
    filename: Optional exact filename filter.
    media_type: Optional MIME type filter (note: most binaries return 'application/octet-stream').

Returns:
    JSON string with list of attachments and metadata.

content_id.description: The ID of the Confluence content (page or blog post) to list attachments for. Example: '123456789'

start.description: (Optional) Starting index for pagination. Use 0 for the first page. To get the next page, add the 'limit' value to 'start'. Default: 0

limit.description: (Optional) Maximum number of attachments to return per request (1-100). Use pagination (start/limit) for large attachment lists. Default: 50

filename.description: (Optional) Filter results to only attachments matching this filename. Exact match only. Example: 'report.pdf'

media_type.description: (Optional) Filter by MIME type. **Note**: Confluence API returns 'application/octet-stream' for most binary files (PNG, JPG, PDF) instead of specific MIME types like 'image/png'. For more reliable filtering, use the 'filename' parameter. Examples: 'application/octet-stream' (binary files), 'application/pdf', 'application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.wordprocessingml.document' (for .docx)

tools.name: confluence_download_attachment
tools.description:
Download an attachment from Confluence as an embedded resource.

Returns the attachment content as a base64-encoded embedded resource so
that it is available over the MCP protocol without requiring filesystem
access on the server. Files larger than 50 MB are not downloaded inline;
a descriptive error message is returned instead.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    attachment_id: The ID of the attachment.

Returns:
    An EmbeddedResource with base64-encoded content, or a TextContent
    with an error or size-exceeded message.

attachment_id.description: The ID of the attachment to download (e.g., 'att123456789'). Find attachment IDs using get_attachments tool. Example workflow: get_attachments(content_id) → use returned ID here.

tools.name: confluence_download_content_attachments
tools.description:
Download all attachments for a Confluence content item as embedded resources.

Returns attachment contents as base64-encoded embedded resources so that
they are available over the MCP protocol without requiring filesystem
access on the server. Files larger than 50 MB are skipped with an error
entry in the summary.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    content_id: The ID of the content.

Returns:
    A list with a text summary followed by one EmbeddedResource per
    successfully downloaded attachment.

content_id.description: The ID of the Confluence content (page or blog post) to download attachments from. Example: '123456789'

tools.name: confluence_delete_attachment
tools.description:
Permanently delete an attachment from Confluence.

**Warning**: This action cannot be undone! The attachment and ALL its versions will be
permanently deleted.

Use this tool to:
- Remove outdated or incorrect attachments
- Clean up duplicate files
- Delete sensitive information that was accidentally uploaded

Best practices:
- Verify the attachment ID before deletion using get_attachments
- Consider downloading the attachment first as a backup
- Check with content owners before deleting shared attachments

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    attachment_id: The ID of the attachment to delete.

Returns:
    JSON string confirming deletion with attachment ID.

attachment_id.description: The ID of the attachment to delete. Attachment IDs can be found using the get_attachments tool. Example: 'att123456789'. **Warning**: This permanently deletes the attachment and all its versions.

tools.name: confluence_get_page_images
tools.description:
Get all images attached to a Confluence page as inline image content.

Filters attachments to images only (PNG, JPEG, GIF, WebP, SVG, BMP)
and returns them as base64-encoded ImageContent that clients can
render directly. Non-image attachments are excluded.

Files with ambiguous MIME types (application/octet-stream) are
detected by filename extension as a fallback. Images larger than
50 MB are skipped with an error entry in the summary.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    content_id: The ID of the content.

Returns:
    A list with a text summary followed by one ImageContent per
    successfully downloaded image.

content_id.description: The ID of the Confluence page or blog post to retrieve images from. Example: '123456789'

Use this tool to:

- Clean up duplicate files
- Delete sensitive information that was accidentally uploaded

Best practices:
- Verify the attachment ID before deletion using get_attachments
- Consider downloading the attachment first as a backup
- Check with content owners before deleting shared attachments

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    attachment_id: The ID of the attachment to delete.

Returns:
    JSON string confirming deletion with attachment ID.

Delete an existing Confluence page.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    page_id: The ID of the page to delete.

Returns:
    JSON string indicating success or failure.

Raises:
    ValueError: If Confluence client is not configured or available.

Download an attachment from Confluence as an embedded resource.

Returns the attachment content as a base64-encoded embedded resource so
that it is available over the MCP protocol without requiring filesystem
access on the server. Files larger than 50 MB are not downloaded inline;
a descriptive error message is returned instead.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    attachment_id: The ID of the attachment.

Returns:
    An EmbeddedResource with base64-encoded content, or a TextContent
    with an error or size-exceeded message.

Download all attachments for a Confluence content item as embedded resources.

Returns attachment contents as base64-encoded embedded resources so that
they are available over the MCP protocol without requiring filesystem
access on the server. Files larger than 50 MB are skipped with an error
entry in the summary.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    content_id: The ID of the content.

Returns:
    A list with a text summary followed by one EmbeddedResource per
    successfully downloaded attachment.

List all attachments for a Confluence content item (page or blog post).

Returns metadata about attachments including:
- Attachment ID, title, and file type
- File size and download URL
- Creation/modification dates
- Version information

**Important**: Confluence API returns 'application/octet-stream' as the media type
for most binary files (PNG, JPG, PDF) instead of specific types like 'image/png'.
For filtering by file type, using the 'filename' parameter is more reliable
(e.g., filename='*.png' pattern matching if supported, or exact filename).

Useful for:
- Discovering what files are attached to a page
- Getting attachment IDs for download operations
- Checking if a specific file exists
- Listing images/documents for processing

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    content_id: The ID of the content.
    start: Starting index for pagination.
    limit: Maximum number of results (1-100).
    filename: Optional exact filename filter.
    media_type: Optional MIME type filter (note: most binaries return 'application/octet-stream').

Returns:
    JSON string with list of attachments and metadata.

Get comments for a specific Confluence page.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    page_id: Confluence page ID.

Returns:
    JSON string representing a list of comment objects.

Get labels for Confluence content (pages, blog posts, or attachments).

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    page_id: Confluence content ID (page or attachment).

Returns:
    JSON string representing a list of label objects.

Get content of a specific Confluence page by its ID, or by its title and space key.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    page_id: Confluence page ID. If provided, 'title' and 'space_key' are ignored.
    title: The exact title of the page. Must be used with 'space_key'.
    space_key: The key of the space. Must be used with 'title'.
    include_metadata: Whether to include page metadata.
    convert_to_markdown: Convert content to markdown (true) or keep raw HTML (false).

Returns:
    JSON string representing the page content and/or metadata, or an error if not found or parameters are invalid.

Get child pages and folders of a specific Confluence page.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    parent_id: The ID of the parent page.
    expand: Fields to expand.
    limit: Maximum number of child items.
    include_content: Whether to include page content.
    convert_to_markdown: Convert content to markdown if include_content is true.
    start: Starting index for pagination.
    include_folders: Whether to include child folders (default: True).

Returns:
    JSON string representing a list of child page and folder objects.

Get a unified diff between two versions of a Confluence page.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    page_id: Confluence page ID.
    from_version: Source version number.
    to_version: Target version number.

Returns:
    JSON string with page info and unified diff.

Get a historical version of a specific Confluence page.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    page_id: Confluence page ID.
    version: The version number to retrieve.

Returns:

Get all images attached to a Confluence page as inline image content.

Filters attachments to images only (PNG, JPEG, GIF, WebP, SVG, BMP)
and returns them as base64-encoded ImageContent that clients can
render directly. Non-image attachments are excluded.

Files with ambiguous MIME types (application/octet-stream) are
detected by filename extension as a fallback. Images larger than
50 MB are skipped with an error entry in the summary.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    content_id: The ID of the content.

Returns:
    A list with a text summary followed by one ImageContent per
    successfully downloaded image.

Get view statistics for a Confluence page.

Note: This tool is only available for Confluence Cloud. Server/Data Center
instances do not support the Analytics API.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    page_id: The Confluence page ID.
    include_title: Whether to include the page title in the response.

Returns:
    JSON string with page view statistics including total views and last viewed date.

Get page hierarchy for a Confluence space as a flat list.

Returns pages with parent_id and depth attributes for token-efficient
processing. Filter by depth to focus on relevant sections, or find
pages by title. Much more efficient than rendering full ASCII trees.

Use this to understand space organization before creating/moving pages.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    space_key: Space key identifier.
    limit: Maximum pages to fetch (start with 100 for faster results).

Returns:
    JSON with space_key, total_pages, and pages array containing
    {id, title, parent_id, position, depth} for each page.
    Root pages have parent_id: null and depth: 0.

Move a Confluence page to a new parent or space.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    page_id: The ID of the page to move.
    target_parent_id: Target parent page ID.
    target_space_key: Target space key for cross-space moves.
    position: Position relative to target ('append', 'above', or 'below').

Returns:
    JSON string representing the moved page object.

Raises:
    ValueError: If neither target_parent_id nor target_space_key
        is provided, or if Confluence client is not configured.

Upload an attachment to Confluence content (page or blog post).

If the attachment already exists (same filename), a new version is created.
This is useful for:
- Attaching documents, images, or files to a page
- Updating existing attachments with new versions
- Adding supporting materials to documentation

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    content_id: The ID of the content to attach to.
    file_path: Path to the file to upload.
    comment: Optional comment for the attachment.
    minor_edit: Whether this is a minor edit (no notifications).

Returns:
    JSON string with upload confirmation and attachment metadata.

Upload multiple attachments to Confluence content in a single operation.

More efficient than calling upload_attachment multiple times. If files with the
same names exist, new versions are created automatically.

Useful for:
- Bulk uploading documentation assets (diagrams, screenshots, etc.)
- Adding multiple related files to a page at once
- Batch updating existing attachments with new versions

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    content_id: The ID of the content to attach to.
    file_paths: List of file paths to upload.
    comment: Optional comment for the attachments.
    minor_edit: Whether this is a minor edit.

Returns:
    JSON string with upload results for each file.

Get allowed option values for a custom field.

Server/DC: Uses createmeta to get allowedValues. Requires project_key
and issue_type parameters.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    field_id: The custom field ID.
    context_id: Field context ID (Cloud only, auto-resolved if omitted).
    project_key: Project key (required for Server/DC).
    issue_type: Issue type name (required for Server/DC).
    contains: Case-insensitive substring filter on option values.
    return_limit: Cap on number of results after filtering.
    values_only: Return compact format with only value strings.

Returns:
    JSON string with the list of available options.

Calculate SLA metrics for a Jira issue.

Computes various time-based metrics including cycle time, lead time,
time spent in each status, due date compliance, and more.

Working hours can be configured via environment variables:
- JIRA_SLA_WORKING_HOURS_ONLY: Enable working hours filtering (true/false)
- JIRA_SLA_WORKING_HOURS_START: Start time (e.g., "09:00")
- JIRA_SLA_WORKING_HOURS_END: End time (e.g., "17:00")
- JIRA_SLA_WORKING_DAYS: Working days (e.g., "1,2,3,4,5" for Mon-Fri)
- JIRA_SLA_TIMEZONE: Timezone for calculations (e.g., "America/New_York")

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    issue_key: The Jira issue key.
    metrics: Comma-separated list of metrics to calculate.
    working_hours_only: Use working hours only for calculations.
    include_raw_dates: Include raw date values in response.

Returns:
    JSON string with calculated SLA metrics.

Update form field answers using the Jira Forms REST API.

This is the primary method for updating form data. Each answer object
must specify the question ID, answer type, and value.

**⚠️ KNOWN LIMITATION - DATETIME fields:**
The Jira Forms API does NOT properly preserve time components in DATETIME fields.
Only the date portion is stored; times are reset to midnight (00:00:00).

**Workaround for DATETIME fields:**
Use jira_update_issue to directly update the underlying custom fields instead:
1. Get the custom field ID from the form details (question's "jiraField" property)
2. Use jira_update_issue with fields like: {"customfield_XXXXX": "2026-01-09T11:50:00-08:00"}

Example:
```python
# Instead of updating via form (loses time):
# jira_update_proforma_form_answers(issue_key, form_id, [{"questionId": "91", "type": "DATETIME", "value": "..."}])

# Use direct field update (preserves time):
jira_update_issue(issue_key, {"customfield_10542": "2026-01-09T11:50:00-08:00"})
```

**Automatic DateTime Conversion:**
For DATE and DATETIME fields, you can provide values as:
- ISO 8601 strings (e.g., "2024-12-17T19:00:00Z", "2024-12-17")
- Unix timestamps in milliseconds (e.g., 1734465600000)

The tool automatically converts ISO 8601 strings to Unix timestamps.

Example answers:
[
    {"questionId": "q1", "type": "TEXT", "value": "Updated description"},
    {"questionId": "q2", "type": "SELECT", "value": "Product A"},
    {"questionId": "q3", "type": "NUMBER", "value": 42},
    {"questionId": "q4", "type": "DATE", "value": "2024-12-17"}
]

Common answer types:
- TEXT: String values
- NUMBER: Numeric values
- DATE: Date values (ISO 8601 string or Unix timestamp in ms)
- DATETIME: DateTime values - ⚠️ USE WORKAROUND ABOVE
- SELECT: Single selection from options
- MULTI_SELECT: Multiple selections (value as list)
- CHECKBOX: Boolean values

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    issue_key: The issue key containing the form.
    form_id: The form UUID (get from get_issue_proforma_forms).
    answers: List of answer objects with questionId, type, and value.

Returns:
    JSON string with operation result.

tools.name: jira_update_proforma_form_answers
tools.description:
Update form field answers using the Jira Forms REST API.

This is the primary method for updating form data. Each answer object
must specify the question ID, answer type, and value.

**⚠️ KNOWN LIMITATION - DATETIME fields:**
The Jira Forms API does NOT properly preserve time components in DATETIME fields.
Only the date portion is stored; times are reset to midnight (00:00:00).

**Workaround for DATETIME fields:**
Use jira_update_issue to directly update the underlying custom fields instead:
1. Get the custom field ID from the form details (question's "jiraField" property)
2. Use jira_update_issue with fields like: {"customfield_XXXXX": "2026-01-09T11:50:00-08:00"}

Example:
```python
# Instead of updating via form (loses time):
# jira_update_proforma_form_answers(issue_key, form_id, [{"questionId": "91", "type": "DATETIME", "value": "..."}])

# Use direct field update (preserves time):
jira_update_issue(issue_key, {"customfield_10542": "2026-01-09T11:50:00-08:00"})
```

**Automatic DateTime Conversion:**
For DATE and DATETIME fields, you can provide values as:
- ISO 8601 strings (e.g., "2024-12-17T19:00:00Z", "2024-12-17")
- Unix timestamps in milliseconds (e.g., 1734465600000)

The tool automatically converts ISO 8601 strings to Unix timestamps.

Example answers:
[
    {"questionId": "q1", "type": "TEXT", "value": "Updated description"},
    {"questionId": "q2", "type": "SELECT", "value": "Product A"},
    {"questionId": "q3", "type": "NUMBER", "value": 42},
    {"questionId": "q4", "type": "DATE", "value": "2024-12-17"}
]

Common answer types:
- TEXT: String values
- NUMBER: Numeric values
- DATE: Date values (ISO 8601 string or Unix timestamp in ms)
- DATETIME: DateTime values - ⚠️ USE WORKAROUND ABOVE
- SELECT: Single selection from options
- MULTI_SELECT: Multiple selections (value as list)
- CHECKBOX: Boolean values

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    issue_key: The issue key containing the form.
    form_id: The form UUID (get from get_issue_proforma_forms).
    answers: List of answer objects with questionId, type, and value.

Returns:
    JSON string with operation result.

answers.description: List of answer objects. Each answer must have: questionId (string), type (TEXT/NUMBER/SELECT/etc), value (any)

- JIRA_SLA_WORKING_HOURS_START: Start time (e.g., "09:00")
- JIRA_SLA_WORKING_HOURS_END: End time (e.g., "17:00")
- JIRA_SLA_WORKING_DAYS: Working days (e.g., "1,2,3,4,5" for Mon-Fri)
- JIRA_SLA_TIMEZONE: Timezone for calculations (e.g., "America/New_York")

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    issue_key: The Jira issue key.
    metrics: Comma-separated list of metrics to calculate.
    working_hours_only: Use working hours only for calculations.
    include_raw_dates: Include raw date values in response.

Returns:
    JSON string with calculated SLA metrics.

metrics.description: Comma-separated list of SLA metrics to calculate. Available: cycle_time, lead_time, time_in_status, due_date_compliance, resolution_time, first_response_time. Defaults to configured metrics or 'cycle_time,time_in_status'.

working_hours_only.description: Calculate using working hours only (excludes weekends/non-business hours). Defaults to value from JIRA_SLA_WORKING_HOURS_ONLY environment variable.

include_raw_dates.description: Include raw date values in the response

tools.name: confluence_search
tools.description:
Search Confluence content using simple terms or CQL.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    query: Search query - can be simple text or a CQL query string.
    limit: Maximum number of results (1-50).
    spaces_filter: Comma-separated list of space keys to filter by.

Returns:
    JSON string representing a list of simplified Confluence page objects.

query.description:
Search query - can be either a simple text (e.g. 'project documentation') or a CQL query string. Simple queries use 'siteSearch' by default, to mimic the WebUI search, with an automatic fallback to 'text' search if not supported. Examples of CQL:
- Basic search: 'type=page AND space=DEV'
- Personal space search: 'space="~username"' (note: personal space keys starting with ~ must be quoted)
- Search by title: 'title~"Meeting Notes"'
- Use siteSearch: 'siteSearch ~ "important concept"'
- Use text search: 'text ~ "important concept"'
- Recent content: 'created >= "2023-01-01"'
- Content with specific label: 'label=documentation'
- Recently modified content: 'lastModified > startOfMonth("-1M")'
- Content modified this year: 'creator = currentUser() AND lastModified > startOfYear()'
- Content you contributed to recently: 'contributor = currentUser() AND lastModified > startOfWeek()'
- Content watched by user: 'watcher = "user@domain.com" AND type = page'
- Exact phrase in content: 'text ~ "\"Urgent Review Required\"" AND label = "pending-approval"'
- Title wildcards: 'title ~ "Minutes*" AND (space = "HR" OR space = "Marketing")'
Note: Special identifiers need proper quoting in CQL: personal space keys (e.g., "~username"), reserved words, numeric IDs, and identifiers with special characters.

spaces_filter.description: (Optional) Comma-separated list of space keys to filter results by. Overrides the environment variable CONFLUENCE_SPACES_FILTER if provided. Use empty string to disable filtering.

tools.name: confluence_get_page
tools.description:
Get content of a specific Confluence page by its ID, or by its title and space key.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    page_id: Confluence page ID. If provided, 'title' and 'space_key' are ignored.
    title: The exact title of the page. Must be used with 'space_key'.
    space_key: The key of the space. Must be used with 'title'.
    include_metadata: Whether to include page metadata.
    convert_to_markdown: Convert content to markdown (true) or keep raw HTML (false).

Returns:
    JSON string representing the page content and/or metadata, or an error if not found or parameters are invalid.

page_id.description: Confluence page ID (numeric ID, can be found in the page URL). For example, in the URL 'https://example.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/TEAM/pages/123456789/Page+Title', the page ID is '123456789'. Provide this OR both 'title' and 'space_key'. If page_id is provided, title and space_key will be ignored.

title.description: The exact title of the Confluence page. Use this with 'space_key' if 'page_id' is not known.

space_key.description: The key of the Confluence space where the page resides (e.g., 'DEV', 'TEAM'). Required if using 'title'.

include_metadata.description: Whether to include page metadata such as creation date, last update, version, and labels.

convert_to_markdown.description: Whether to convert page to markdown (true) or keep it in raw HTML format (false). Raw HTML can reveal macros (like dates) not visible in markdown, but CAUTION: using HTML significantly increases token usage in AI responses.

    start: Starting index for pagination.
    include_folders: Whether to include child folders (default: True).

Returns:
    JSON string representing a list of child page and folder objects.

parent_id.description: The ID of the parent page whose children you want to retrieve

expand.description: Fields to expand in the response (e.g., 'version', 'body.storage')

limit.description: Maximum number of child items to return (1-50)

include_content.description: Whether to include the page content in the response

convert_to_markdown.description: Whether to convert page content to markdown (true) or keep it in raw HTML format (false). Only relevant if include_content is true.

include_folders.description: Whether to include child folders in addition to child pages

tools.name: confluence_get_space_page_tree
tools.description:
Get page hierarchy for a Confluence space as a flat list.

Returns pages with parent_id and depth attributes for token-efficient

pages by title. Much more efficient than rendering full ASCII trees.

Use this to understand space organization before creating/moving pages.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    space_key: Space key identifier.
    limit: Maximum pages to fetch (start with 100 for faster results).

Returns:
    JSON with space_key, total_pages, and pages array containing
    {id, title, parent_id, position, depth} for each page.
    Root pages have parent_id: null and depth: 0.

name.description: Label name to add (lowercase, no spaces). Examples: 'draft', 'reviewed', 'confidential', 'v1.0'. Labels help organize and categorize content.

tools.name: confluence_create_page
tools.description:
Create a new Confluence page.

Args:
    ctx: The FastMCP context.
    space_key: The key of the space.
    title: The title of the page.
    content: The content of the page (format depends on content_format).
    parent_id: Optional parent page ID.
    content_format: The format of the content ('markdown', 'wiki', or 'storage').
    enable_heading_anchors: Whether to enable heading anchors (markdown only).
    include_content: Whether to include page content in the response.
    emoji: Optional page title emoji (icon shown in navigation).

Returns:
    JSON string representing the created page object.

Raises:
    ValueError: If in read-only mode, Confluence client is unavailable, or invalid content_format.

space_key.description: The key of the space to create the page in (usually a short uppercase code like 'DEV', 'TEAM', or 'DOC')

content.description: The content of the page. Format depends on content_format parameter. Can be Markdown (default), wiki markup, or storage format

parent_id.description: (Optional) parent page ID. If provided, this page will be created as a child of the specified page

content_format.description: (Optional) The format of the content parameter. Options: 'markdown' (default), 'wiki', or 'storage'. Wiki format uses Confluence wiki markup syntax

enable_heading_anchors.description: (Optional) Whether to enable automatic heading anchor generation. Only applies when content_format is 'markdown'

include_content.description: (Optional) Whether to include page content in the response. Defaults to false since callers already have the content at create time

emoji.description: (Optional) Page title emoji (icon shown in navigation). Can be any emoji character like '📝', '🚀', '📚'. Set to null/None to remove.

Update an existing Confluence page.

    include_content: Whether to include page content in the response.
    emoji: Optional page title emoji (icon shown in navigation).

Returns:
    JSON string representing the updated page object.

Raises:
    ValueError: If Confluence client is not configured, available, or invalid content_format.

page_id.description: The ID of the page to update

title.description: The new title of the page

content.description: The new content of the page. Format depends on content_format parameter

is_minor_edit.description: Whether this is a minor edit

parent_id.description: Optional the new parent page ID

content_format.description: (Optional) The format of the content parameter. Options: 'markdown' (default), 'wiki', or 'storage'. Wiki format uses Confluence wiki markup syntax

enable_heading_anchors.description: (Optional) Whether to enable automatic heading anchor generation. Only applies when content_format is 'markdown'

include_content.description: (Optional) Whether to include page content in the response. Defaults to false since callers already have the content at update time

emoji.description: (Optional) Page title emoji (icon shown in navigation). Can be any emoji character like '📝', '🚀', '📚'. Set to null/None to remove.
